summary: Argentine author known for labyrinthine, philosophical short stories collected in Ficciones and El Aleph. Major stories include 'The Aleph' (a point containing all other points), 'The Library of Babel' (an infinite library of hexagonal rooms), 'The Garden of Forking Paths' (the spy Yu Tsun and a labyrinth-novel), 'Tlön, Uqbar, Orbis Tertius' (an encyclopedia of a fictional idealist world), 'Pierre Menard, Author of the Quixote,' 'Death and the Compass' (Erik Lönnrot vs. Red Scharlach), 'Funes the Memorious' (perfect memory after a fall), and 'The Circular Ruins' (dreaming a man into existence). Also a poet and essayist. Went blind. Collaborated with Adolfo Bioy Casares. Published Cortázar's first story. Deeply influenced by Kafka, whom he translated. Known for interest in labyrinths, mirrors, infinity, and detective fiction.

works.name: The Aleph
works.description: A story in which the narrator (named 'Borges') mourns Beatriz Viterbo and visits the cellar of her cousin, the poet Carlos Argentino Daneri, who is writing a poem called 'The Earth' describing every inch of the planet. In the cellar, lying on the nineteenth step of the staircase, the narrator sees the title object: a point in space containing all other points. He sees 'a silvery cobweb in the center of a black pyramid' and 'convex equatorial deserts.' Wikipedia

works.name: The Library of Babel
works.description: A story imagining a universe consisting of an indefinite number of hexagonal galleries/rooms containing every possible combination of letters in book form. Most books are meaningless. 'Purifiers' destroy books searching for the 'Crimson Hexagon.' Inspired by the infinite monkey theorem and Kurt Lasswitz's Traumkristalle. Epigraph from Robert Burton about 'the variation of 23 letters.' Wikipedia

people search for the 'Crimson Hexagon' in a structure that contains almost entirely meaningless books

examples: 'Purifiers' who search for the 'Crimson Hexagon'
this author wrote about 'purifiers' who destroy books in an infinite collection

clues.clue: Inspired by the infinite monkey theorem; Lasswitz's Traumkristalle; epigraph from Burton about '23 letters'
examples: inspired by the infinite monkey theorem
This writer credits Kurt Lasswitz's Traumkristalle with inspiring one of his stories, whose epigraph is Robert Burton's line

works.name: The Garden of Forking Paths
works.description: A spy story in which the German agent Yu Tsun escapes Richard Madden, visits the Sinologist Stephen Albert, and shoots him to communicate the name of a city to German intelligence. Albert has discovered that Yu Tsun's ancestor Ts'ui Pen's 'unfinished novel' and 'labyrinth' are one and the same — a book exploring all possible outcomes. The labyrinth is analogized to a guessing game where the answer is 'chess.' Written for the centenary of Poe's 'Murders in the Rue Morgue.' May anticipate the Many Worlds interpretation of quantum mechanics. Inspired Stuart Moulthrop's hypertext fiction. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Yu Tsun shoots Stephen Albert to telegraph the name of a city to bomb; escapes Richard Madden

works.name: Tlön, Uqbar, Orbis Tertius
works.description: A story in which the narrator (Borges) and Bioy Casares discover an encyclopedia describing the fictional land of Uqbar and the idealist world of Tlön. Its language has no nouns; one word means 'to moonate.' 'Mirrors and copulation are abominable, since they both multiply the numbers of men.' The society is founded on Berkeley's idealism. American millionaire Ezra Buckley finances a full encyclopedia. Ends with Borges translating Browne's Urn Burial. Wikipedia

clues.clue: An encyclopedia describes a fictional idealist society with a nounless language; 'hlor u fang axaxaxas mlo' means 'to moonate'

works.name: Pierre Menard, Author of the Quixote
works.description: A story in which the fictional French writer Pierre Menard recreates Don Quixote word-for-word, not by copying but by 'being' Cervantes. The narrator considers Menard's identical version superior to the original. Menard's few surviving works include diatribes against Paul Valéry. Wikipedia

works.name: Death and the Compass
works.description: A detective story in which Erik Lönnrot investigates murders that form the Tetragrammaton pattern. He ascends the villa of Triste-le-Roy. The criminal Red Scharlach traps and kills Lönnrot at the fourth point of the pattern. A rabbi is killed on December 3. Wikipedia

works.name: Funes the Memorious
works.description: A story about Ireneo Funes, a boy who gains perfect memory after falling from a horse. He gives each number up to 24,000 an arbitrary name (e.g., 'Maximo Perez' for certain numbers). He is irritated that the same noun indicates a dog seen at different times. He cannot generalize or abstract. Wikipedia

works.name: The Circular Ruins
works.description: A story about a wizard who dreams a man into existence in a ruined temple dedicated to fire. In the twist ending, the wizard is unharmed by fire, meaning he too was dreamed into existence. Wikipedia

works.name: The Book of Sand
works.description: A story about a book with no beginning or end, written in an unknown language. The narrator trades his black-letter Wycliffe Bible for it. He sees a picture of an anchor but can never find the same page again. Wikipedia

works.name: Other Stories
works.description: Other frequently clued stories: 'Borges and I' (ends 'I do not know which of us has written this page'), 'The South' (Juan Dahlmann, knife fight on the plain), 'Emma Zunz' (shoots Loewenthal, claims rape), 'Three Versions of Judas' (God incarnated as Judas), 'The Immortal' (Marcus Flaminius Rufus, Homer as a troglodyte), 'The Zahir' (an obsessive coin, a tiger painting), 'Deutsches Requiem' (SS officer Otto zur Linde), 'On Exactitude in Science' (a map at the scale of a mile to the mile), 'Theme of the Hero and the Traitor' (Fergus Kilpatrick), 'Averroës' Search' (Averroës doesn't know what a play is), 'The Approach to Al-Mu'tasim.'

works.name: Poetry and Essays
works.description: Borges as poet and essayist. 'Poem of the Gifts': 'I had always imagined Paradise as a kind of library.' 'A Rose and Milton': a flower 'from some obliterated garden' that Milton held but could not see. 'General Quiroga Rides to His Death in a Carriage.' 'Kafka and His Precursors': writers create their own precursors. 'The Analytical Language of John Wilkins': a Chinese classification system including 'those that from afar look like flies.' 'Blindness' essay. A Universal History of Infamy. The Book of Imaginary Beings (120 mythical creatures, A Bao A Qu, Zaratan). Dreamtigers. In Praise of Darkness. Collaborated with Bioy Casares (H. Bustos Domecq pseudonym). Published Cortázar's 'House Taken Over.' Interested in Old English / Anglo-Saxon (tombstone inscribed with 'The Battle of Maldon').

comprehensive_summary:
Jorge Luis Borges (1899–1986) is an Argentine author known for labyrinthine, philosophical short stories collected in Ficciones and El Aleph, as well as poetry and essays.

His most frequently clued story is 'The Aleph,' in which the narrator (named Borges) mourns Beatriz Viterbo and visits the cellar of her cousin Carlos Argentino Daneri, a poet writing 'The Earth' to describe every inch of the planet. Lying on the nineteenth step of the cellar staircase, the narrator sees the Aleph: a point in space containing all other points, including 'a silvery cobweb in the center of a black pyramid' and 'convex equatorial deserts.' Daneri follows 'verbal ostentation,' inventing the word 'lactinacious' for 'milky.'

'The Library of Babel' imagines a universe of hexagonal galleries containing every possible combination of letters in book form. Most books are meaningless. 'Purifiers' search for the 'Crimson Hexagon.' The story was inspired by the infinite monkey theorem and Lasswitz's Traumkristalle, with an epigraph from Robert Burton about 'the variation of 23 letters.'

'The Garden of Forking Paths' follows the German spy Yu Tsun, who escapes Richard Madden, visits the Sinologist Stephen Albert, and shoots him to communicate a city's name to German intelligence. Albert discovers that Yu Tsun's ancestor Ts'ui Pen's 'novel' and 'labyrinth' are one and the same, a book exploring all possible outcomes, analogized to a guessing game where the answer is 'chess.' The story may anticipate the Many Worlds interpretation of quantum mechanics.

'Tlön, Uqbar, Orbis Tertius' features the narrator and Bioy Casares discovering an encyclopedia of the fictional world of Tlön, founded on Berkeley's idealism, with a nounless language where 'hlor u fang axaxaxas mlo' means 'to moonate.' The heresiarch declares 'mirrors and copulation are abominable.' Millionaire Ezra Buckley finances the encyclopedia on condition it 'makes no pact with the impostor Jesus Christ.' The story ends with Borges translating Browne's Urn Burial.

'Pierre Menard, Author of the Quixote' describes a fictional French writer who recreates Don Quixote word-for-word by 'being' Cervantes. The narrator considers Menard's version superior. Menard's surviving works include diatribes against Valéry.

'Death and the Compass' is a detective story where Erik Lönnrot investigates murders forming the Tetragrammaton pattern. Red Scharlach traps and kills Lönnrot at the fourth point. Lönnrot ascends the villa of Triste-le-Roy.

'Funes the Memorious' concerns Ireneo Funes, who gains perfect memory after falling from a horse. He assigns arbitrary names to numbers, is irritated that different temporal views of a dog share one noun, and cannot generalize.

'The Circular Ruins': a wizard dreams a man into existence; in the twist, the wizard himself was dreamed. 'The Book of Sand': a book with no beginning or end, traded for a Wycliffe Bible. 'Borges and I' ends: 'I do not know which of us has written this page.' 'The South': Juan Dahlmann accepts a knife fight. 'Emma Zunz': Emma shoots Loewenthal to avenge her father. 'Three Versions of Judas': God incarnated as Judas. 'On Exactitude in Science': a map at mile-to-mile scale. 'Deutsches Requiem': SS officer Otto zur Linde. 'Averroës' Search': Averroës can't translate Aristotle because he doesn't know what a play is. 'The Immortal': a troglodyte named Argos is Homer.

As a poet: 'Poem of the Gifts' ('I had always imagined Paradise as a kind of library'), 'A Rose and Milton,' 'General Quiroga Rides to His Death in a Carriage.' His essay 'Kafka and His Precursors' argues that writers create their own precursors. The Book of Imaginary Beings describes 120 creatures. He went blind and wrote the essay 'Blindness.' He collaborated with Bioy Casares under the pseudonym H. Bustos Domecq. He published Cortázar's 'House Taken Over.' He was passionate about Old English; his tombstone bears a line from 'The Battle of Maldon.' He translated Kafka into Spanish. His foremost English translators include Alastair Reid and Norman Thomas di Giovanni.

Short Story: this story imagines a universe of hexagonal rooms containing every possible book; 'Purifiers' search for the 'Crimson Hexagon'
Short Story: in this story, the German spy Yu Tsun shoots the Sinologist Stephen Albert to communicate a city's name; Albert discovered that Ts'ui Pen's novel and labyrinth are one and the same
Short Story: in this story, an encyclopedia describes the idealist world of Tlön with a nounless language; 'mirrors and copulation are abominable'
Short Story: in this story, a fictional French writer recreates Don Quixote word-for-word by seeking to 'be' Cervantes; the narrator considers it superior to the original

cards.front: Short Story: in this story, the detective Erik Lönnrot investigates murders forming the Tetragrammaton pattern and is killed by Red Scharlach at the fourth point
Short Story: in this story, Ireneo Funes gains perfect memory after falling from a horse, assigning arbitrary names to numbers and unable to generalize
Short Story: in this story, a wizard dreams a man into existence in a ruined temple; the twist reveals the wizard himself was dreamed (he is unharmed by fire)

recursive_suggestions: The Aleph (short story) — Most heavily clued individual story with many specific detail clues
Ficciones — The collection as a whole contains most of Borges's major stories
Adolfo Bioy Casares — Frequently cross-referenced collaborator
